# Society & Economics

Social movements, economic events, and demographic developments from the year 2007.

<!-- BEGIN GENERATED: category-index — maintained by build-structure; do not edit by hand -->

| Topic | Summary |
|---|---|
| [Subprime Mortgage Crisis and Bear Stearns Hedge Fund Collapse](subprime-mortgage-crisis.md) | The July 2007 collapse of two Bear Stearns subprime hedge funds — losing nearly $1.8 billion in investor capital — marked the moment housing-market stress became visible systemic failure and catalyzed the 2008 global financial crisis. |
| [Ben Bernanke and the Federal Reserve's 2007 Crisis Response](ben-bernanke-fed-2007.md) | As the subprime crisis erupted, Fed Chairman and Great Depression scholar Ben Bernanke cut interest rates 100 basis points, created the Term Auction Facility, and coordinated international liquidity to prevent systemic collapse in 2007. |
| [Hank Paulson and the Treasury Department's 2007 Crisis Response](hank-paulson-treasury-2007.md) | Treasury Secretary and former Goldman Sachs CEO Hank Paulson led the government's early response to the subprime crisis through liquidity coordination, the Hope Now Alliance, and the December 2007 ARM rate-freeze plan. |
| [Stock Market Peaks Before 2008 Crisis](stock-market-peak-october-2007.md) | The Dow Jones Industrial Average closed at an all-time high of 14,164.53 on October 9, 2007, marking the zenith of the 2002–2007 bull market before the global financial crisis erased over half its value. |
| [Oil Prices Spike to Near $100 per Barrel](oil-price-spike-2007.md) | Crude oil reached $99.29 per barrel on November 21, 2007 — the closest prices had ever come to $100 — signaling a structural shift in global energy markets driven by surging demand and constrained supply. |
| [Nuclear Power Renaissance Begins](nuclear-power-renaissance-2007.md) | In 2007, global interest in nuclear power rebounded after two decades of stagnation as climate concern, energy security, and Asian demand spurred new reactor plans — including the first U.S. license applications in 28 years — a revival later reversed by the 2011 Fukushima disaster. |

<!-- END GENERATED: category-index -->
